update_notes: Updated with new 2025-2026 papers: SynthSAEBench (2602.14687), Sparse but Wrong (2508.16560), On the Limits of SAEs (2506.15963), Taming Polysemanticity (2506.14002), Feature Consistency (2505.20254), SAE Feature Manifolds (2509.02565). None undermine cand_a core novelty.

candidates.title: Rate-Distortion Theory + Cross-Domain Characterization + ASI + Phase Transition

collisions.paper: Tang et al. (2025). A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning in Mechanistic Interpretability: Piecewise Biconvexity and Spurious Minima. arXiv:2512.05534. v3 January 2026.
collisions.overlap: Both provide formal theory explaining why feature absorption occurs in SAEs. Tang et al. prove that spurious partial minima exhibiting absorption exist in the piecewise biconvex optimization landscape and establish necessary/sufficient conditions for correct global feature recovery. Our contribution differs: we derive a closed-form geometric threshold (lambda > sin^2(theta_{p,c})) as a function of measurable quantities for predicting which specific feature pairs are at absorption risk — a different question (per-pair prediction vs. existence of spurious minima).

collisions.paper: Tilde Research (2024). The Rate Distortion Dance of Sparse Autoencoders. Blog post at tilderesearch.com/blog/rate-distortion-saes
collisions.overlap: The blog discusses rate-distortion framing of SAEs including the L1/L0 Lagrangian and sparse coding theory. Does NOT address feature absorption specifically or derive a closed-form absorption threshold as a function of decoder angle. Only informal motivation for the framing.

collisions.paper: Bricken et al. (2023/2025). Towards Monosemanticity. Anthropic. (ICLR 2025 version uses decoder cosine similarity to classify latent types)
collisions.overlap: Uses decoder cosine similarity as a heuristic to classify latent types (threshold 0.7 for GPT-2, 0.4 for GemmaScope), with ROC curve analysis. Our ASI differs: (1) pairwise metric for parent-child feature pairs rather than per-feature classifier; (2) includes frequency ratio term freq_p/freq_c to capture hierarchy asymmetry; (3) framed as probe-free absorption susceptibility predictor for unknown feature pairs; (4) validated against Chanin et al. absorption labels (AUROC). Different application, different formula, different validation.

collisions.paper: Cui et al. (2026). On the Limits of Sparse Autoencoders: A Theoretical Framework and Reweighted Remedy. arXiv:2506.15963. ICLR 2026.
collisions.overlap: Provides a closed-form SAE identifiability analysis showing SAEs fail to recover ground truth features unless extremely sparse. Different from our absorption threshold: addresses global feature recovery identifiability (all features), not per-pair absorption prediction given SAE hyperparameters. The reweighting remedy is unrelated to our approach.

collisions.paper: Chanin, Garriga-Alonso (2026). SynthSAEBench: Evaluating Sparse Autoencoders on Scalable Realistic Synthetic Data. arXiv:2602.14687. Feb 2026.
collisions.overlap: Evaluates SAE absorption on synthetic hierarchical features at scale. Key difference: synthetic controlled ground-truth data vs. our real LLM (Gemma Scope) semantic hierarchies with empirical null controls. SynthSAEBench complements our contribution as a synthetic baseline; the cross-domain real-LLM gap remains open.

sub_contributions.claim: Formal rate-distortion proof with closed-form absorption threshold lambda > sin^2(theta_{p,c})
sub_contributions.notes: Informal observation exists; multiple formal theories exist but none derive the per-pair quantitative threshold or the co-occurrence frequency cancellation prediction. Tang et al. 2512.05534 is closest but addresses a different question.

sub_contributions.claim: First cross-domain absorption characterization across spelling, entity-type, geographic, grammatical hierarchies in real LLMs
sub_contributions.notes: SynthSAEBench (2602.14687) covers synthetic hierarchies. SAEBench explicitly acknowledges the real-LLM cross-domain gap. No real-LLM cross-domain absorption measurement with rigorous null controls found in any paper.

sub_contributions.claim: Probe-free Absorption Susceptibility Index (ASI) from decoder geometry and activation frequencies
sub_contributions.notes: Bricken et al. decoder cosine similarity is partial precursor. ASI adds frequency ratio, pairwise framing, and absorption-label validation (AUROC). Song et al. PW-MCC (2505.20254) is a different metric (training consistency, not absorption susceptibility).

sub_contributions.claim: Phase transition and hysteresis characterization of absorption onset
sub_contributions.notes: Zero results for phase-transition framing with functional form fitting or hysteresis testing in SAE absorption context. L0 sweep plots exist qualitatively but no statistical analysis of transition type.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Must explicitly position against: (1) arXiv:2512.05534 — existence of spurious minima vs. per-pair quantitative prediction; (2) arXiv:2602.14687 — synthetic data vs. real-LLM semantic hierarchies; (3) Tilde Blog — informal framing vs. formal derivation with testable predictions; (4) Bricken et al. — per-feature vs. pairwise, no frequency ratio, different validation target. All differentiations are scientifically defensible.

candidates.title: Absorption-as-Representational-Diagnostic

collisions.paper: Song et al. (2025). Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Should Prioritize Feature Consistency in SAEs. arXiv:2505.20254. May 2025.
collisions.overlap: Shows SAE features are only ~0.80 consistent (PW-MCC) across training runs. Weakens the claim that an absorption graph encodes stable model structure. Must address or restrict to canonical Gemma Scope SAEs.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Proceed as fallback only. Must address feature consistency risk (Song et al. 2505.20254) upfront. Restrict claims to canonical single-release Gemma Scope SAEs to control seed dependence. Novelty framing (absorption graph as model representation diagnostic) remains novel.

candidates.title: Systematic Mitigation Benchmark

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al. (2025). SAEBench. arXiv:2503.09532. ICML 2025.
collisions.overlap: Systematically compares Matryoshka and OrtSAE on absorption and other metrics. Partial comparison of architectures already exists.
Evaluates multiple SAE architectures on synthetic hierarchical data including absorption. Further reduces incremental value of a benchmark contribution without theoretical framing.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Only viable as empirical supplement to cand_a. SynthSAEBench (Feb 2026) further reduces the standalone value of a pure benchmark paper.

candidates.title: Feature Anchoring for Real LLM Absorption Reduction

collisions.paper: Tang et al. (2025). A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning in Mechanistic Interpretability: Piecewise Biconvexity and Spurious Minima. arXiv:2512.05534. v3 January 2026.
collisions.overlap: Tang et al. propose feature anchoring as a novel technique and validate its effectiveness with extensive experiments across diverse SDL methods and settings, explicitly including real neural representations. The core contribution of cand_d is already published. The proposal's claim that anchoring is 'currently only validated on synthetic benchmarks' is factually incorrect.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Core contribution already published. Residual novelty (testing anchoring specifically against Chanin absorption metric) is too narrow for a standalone contribution. Drop immediately.

new_papers_incorporated.citation: Chanin, Garriga-Alonso (2026). SynthSAEBench: Evaluating Sparse Autoencoders on Scalable Realistic Synthetic Data. arXiv:2602.14687.
new_papers_incorporated.impact: Must cite as related work. Real-LLM cross-domain gap remains open.
new_papers_incorporated.collision_with: cand_a cross-domain claim — minor partial overlap (synthetic vs. real LLM)

new_papers_incorporated.citation: Chanin, Garriga-Alonso (2025). Sparse but Wrong: Incorrect L0 Leads to Incorrect Features in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2508.16560.
new_papers_incorporated.impact: Must cite; validates our L0 deconfounding control in Phase C.4. Does not undermine any claim.

new_papers_incorporated.citation: Cui et al. (2026). On the Limits of Sparse Autoencoders: A Theoretical Framework and Reweighted Remedy. arXiv:2506.15963.
new_papers_incorporated.impact: Must cite as related theoretical work. Addresses SAE identifiability globally; our absorption threshold addresses per-pair prediction — different questions.
new_papers_incorporated.collision_with: cand_a theory — partial_overlap, but different theoretical question

new_papers_incorporated.citation: Chen et al. (2025). Taming Polysemanticity in LLMs: Provable Feature Recovery via Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2506.14002.
new_papers_incorporated.impact: Related provable method; not absorption-specific. Cite as related theoretical work.

new_papers_incorporated.citation: Song et al. (2025). Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Should Prioritize Feature Consistency in SAEs. arXiv:2505.20254.
new_papers_incorporated.impact: Must cite; PW-MCC of 0.80 for TopK SAEs is a risk for cand_b. Supports using canonical Gemma Scope SAEs for cand_a.

new_papers_incorporated.citation: Michaud, Gorton, McGrath (2025). Understanding sparse autoencoder scaling in the presence of feature manifolds. arXiv:2509.02565. NeurIPS 2025 MI Workshop.
new_papers_incorporated.impact: Geometric analysis showing decoder direction clustering (high cosine similarity between manifold-tiling latents). Partially supports ASI intuition. Cite as related geometric work.

key_related_papers.citation: Chanin et al. (2024). A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507. NeurIPS 2025 Oral.
key_related_papers.relevance: Foundation paper: defines absorption, first-letter task, absorption metric, identifies the problem across many SAEs.

key_related_papers.citation: Tang et al. (2025). A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning in Mechanistic Interpretability: Piecewise Biconvexity and Spurious Minima. arXiv:2512.05534. v3 Jan 2026.
key_related_papers.relevance: Most important related theoretical work. Must differentiate clearly: existence of spurious minima vs. per-pair quantitative prediction threshold.

key_related_papers.citation: Tilde Research (2024). The Rate Distortion Dance of Sparse Autoencoders. Blog.
key_related_papers.relevance: Informal precursor to rate-distortion framing. Must cite and position formal derivation as the extension.

key_related_papers.citation: Bussmann et al. (2025). Learning Multi-Level Features with Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.17547. ICML 2025.
key_related_papers.relevance: Key mitigation paper; validates absorption is measurable and reducible.

key_related_papers.citation: Karvonen et al. (2025). SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders in Language Model Interpretability. arXiv:2503.09532. ICML 2025.
key_related_papers.relevance: Key evaluation infrastructure. Explicitly acknowledges the cross-domain gap we fill.

key_related_papers.citation: Korznikov et al. (2025). OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features. arXiv:2509.22033.
key_related_papers.relevance: Key mitigation comparison in Phase F.

key_related_papers.citation: Chanin, Dulka, Garriga-Alonso (2025). Feature Hedging: Correlated Features Break Narrow Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2505.11756.
key_related_papers.relevance: Related failure mode. Must distinguish absorption from hedging clearly.

key_related_papers.citation: Chanin, Garriga-Alonso (2025). Sparse but Wrong: Incorrect L0 Leads to Incorrect Features in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2508.16560.
key_related_papers.relevance: L0 deconfounding — directly used in Phase C.4 control.

key_related_papers.citation: Chanin, Garriga-Alonso (2026). SynthSAEBench: Evaluating Sparse Autoencoders on Scalable Realistic Synthetic Data. arXiv:2602.14687.
key_related_papers.relevance: Complementary synthetic benchmark; must position our real-LLM cross-domain contribution as filling the remaining gap.

key_related_papers.citation: Cui et al. (2026). On the Limits of Sparse Autoencoders: A Theoretical Framework and Reweighted Remedy. arXiv:2506.15963.
key_related_papers.relevance: Related theoretical work on SAE identifiability; must distinguish from our absorption threshold.

key_related_papers.citation: Song et al. (2025). Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Should Prioritize Feature Consistency in SAEs. arXiv:2505.20254.
key_related_papers.relevance: SAE feature consistency — relevant to experimental design (canonical Gemma Scope SAEs).

key_related_papers.citation: Michaud, Gorton, McGrath (2025). Understanding sparse autoencoder scaling in the presence of feature manifolds. arXiv:2509.02565.
key_related_papers.relevance: Geometric analysis of decoder direction clustering; related to ASI intuition.

key_related_papers.citation: ATM (2025). Adaptive Temporal Masking for Stable Sparse Autoencoder Training. arXiv:2510.08855.
key_related_papers.relevance: Key mitigation: 40% absorption reduction on Gemma-2-2B layer 12 (same as primary setup).

key_related_papers.citation: Tian et al. (2025). Measuring Sparse Autoencoder Feature Sensitivity. arXiv:2509.23717. NeurIPS 2025 Workshop Spotlight.
key_related_papers.relevance: Nearest related metric (feature sensitivity vs. absorption susceptibility). Distinguish ASI from this.

critical_risks.risk: arXiv:2512.05534 (Tang et al.) could be seen as covering rate-distortion theory of absorption
critical_risks.mitigation: The two papers answer different questions. Tang et al. study whether the optimization landscape has spurious minima (existence question). We derive which specific feature pairs will be absorbed given SAE hyperparameters (prediction question). The closed-form threshold lambda > sin^2(theta_{p,c}) is absent from Tang et al. This distinction must be made explicit in the introduction and related work.

critical_risks.risk: arXiv:2602.14687 (SynthSAEBench) could reduce perceived novelty of cross-domain contribution
critical_risks.mitigation: SynthSAEBench uses synthetic data with controlled ground-truth hierarchies. Our contribution is the first measurement of feature absorption across real semantic hierarchy types (entity-type, geographic, grammatical) in a real LLM (Gemma Scope), with empirical null controls (shuffled labels, bootstrap CIs, Bonferroni correction). These are complementary, not competing.

critical_risks.risk: Cand_d is not novel (feature anchoring already published in arXiv:2512.05534 v3)
critical_risks.mitigation: Drop cand_d immediately. Do not list it as a contribution.

critical_risks.risk: ATM paper uses same experimental setup (Gemma-2-2B layer 12, 16k SAE) and achieves 40% absorption reduction
critical_risks.mitigation: Our cross-domain characterization and ASI are training-free and probe-free; ATM requires retraining. Use ATM results as a comparison point in Phase F analysis.

critical_risks.risk: Multiple 2025-2026 theoretical papers on SAE limits could crowd the theory space
critical_risks.mitigation: None of the new theoretical papers (2506.15963, 2506.14002, 2512.05534) derive a geometric per-pair absorption threshold with measurable inputs. Position clearly as complementary to the growing theoretical landscape.
